pages.full_text:
first steam [&] turbine boat, built by Parsons. It had blades like a fan. Now all electricity is made by similar engines. He made models of boats & tested them on his pond.
	Other rooms showed jobs in Newcastle. Coal trimmers went on the coal boats to level the loads off in the hold. They had a shovel & wore knee caps. 90% cargoes were coal. The jobs were handed on father→Son.
	In the evening we walked to a good restaurant near Peter's house, Casa Mia, but the service was slow. We had a maincourse, & delicious puddings.

Sunday
	It was damp & cold. The milk was frozen & I ate it, crunching in my cereal for breakfast. Then I walked the dog slowly & sat in the back of the van (cold) to South Shields. Although I was wearing 2 sweaters & a coat, when Simon suggested we look at Tugs & ferries, I went to
pages.notes: Text continues from a previous page mid-sentence. The left page has no visible handwritten page number. The restaurant 'Casa Mia' is near someone named Peter's house. The newspaper clipping on the right page mentions 'Cape Trafalgar 200 years ago', suggesting this trip occurred around 2005.

pages.full_text:
News reached France 3 months after the greatest naval victory. "Le Moniteur" briefly reported: "A storm has caused us the loss of a few ships after an imprudently delivered battle."

[NEWSPAPER CLIPPING PASTED IN - "WEATHER EYE PAUL SIMONS: Nelson was canny with the weather"]

look at Woolworths, to warm up. Then we had a warming lunch in a pub £3.95 each. (chil. wd. have been £7.95) Roast beef yorkshire pud & veg & gravy, followed by black berry & apple crumble & custard.
	Afterwards we tackled the Museum in
photo_captions: WEATHER EYE PAUL SIMONS: Nelson was canny with the weather
pages.notes: The right page has a handwritten page number '27' in the top-right corner. A newspaper clipping is pasted in, headlined 'Nelson was canny with the weather' by Paul Simons (WEATHER EYE column). The clipping text discusses Cape Trafalgar 200 years ago, placing this in approximately 2005. The clipping text reads: 'ALTHOUGH the events off Cape Trafalgar 200 years ago took place in light winds, the opposing fleets were wallowing in an ominous westerly swell. Nelson, realising that a gale was blowing in from the Atlantic, knew that he had little time to press home his attack. The swell gradually strengthened, but fortunately for the British fleet the great battle was won when the storm finally arrived. Ships were hurled around in fierce winds and seas, and many of the captured enemy vessels were lost. Dennis Wheeler, at the University of Sunderland, has reconstructed the weather at the time. Reporting in the Royal Meteorological Society's journal Weather, he estimates that the Atlantic coasts of Spain and France were blasted by a two-week-long storm, one of the worst storms to hit the region in the 19th century. In these horrendous conditions the tiny schooner HMS Pickle was dispatched to England with the news of Trafalgar. During the nerve-racking 1,100-mile journey Pickle almost sank in hurricane-force winds and had to jettison her guns. She arrived, badly battered at Falmouth on November 4. Her captain, Lieutenant John Lapenotiere, reached the Admiralty in London the following day, after a 36-hour journey and 21 changes of horses, to deliver the momentous news of the victory and the death of Nelson — a fortnight after the event.' The handwritten text around and below the clipping (left margin) quotes from 'Le Moniteur' about the naval victory. This is part of the same Sunday trip to South Shields, continuing from the left page.
